hook: You already know how to fit a regression line and interpret its slope — but how do you know if that relationship is real or just a fluke of your sample? A slope of 2.3 might look meaningful, yet without the right tools you can't tell signal from noise. In this unit, you'll learn to rigorously test and quantify relationships between variables, turning a line on a scatterplot into a trustworthy, defensible conclusion.
concept: The unifying idea is using sample data to draw confident, honest conclusions about the true linear relationship between two variables — formally known as *inference for the simple linear regression model* $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \varepsilon$.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Testing the Slope of a Regression Line
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to conduct a $t$-test on the regression slope $\beta_1$ to determine whether a linear relationship between two variables is statistically significant or could simply be due to chance.

Master the construction and interpretation of confidence intervals for the true slope $\beta_1$, giving you a range of plausible values that quantifies how precisely your data pin down the relationship.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Residual Analysis and Conditions for Inference
topic_roadmap.description: Apply residual plots and diagnostic checks to verify that the conditions of linearity, equal variance, independence, and normality are satisfied before trusting any inference from your regression model.

You will connect the statistical reasoning developed throughout this unit to real-world decisions, seeing how probability quantifies uncertainty and guides choices in contexts ranging from medicine to business.
